segments.title: Why commanders stood in exposed positions
segments.summary: From Mikasa's command position, Dr. Clarke explains why admirals and commanding officers accepted exposure in battle before radar and electronic sensors.
segments.body: The command position on Mikasa poses a stark question: why would senior officers place themselves where battle made them especially vulnerable? Dr. Clarke's answer is information. Before radar and electronic sensing, the best way to understand a fast-changing battle was often direct observation from a position with the widest possible view. A protected command room could receive reports from lookouts, but a commanding officer who wanted to make the most informed decisions might choose to see the situation personally. He frames that choice as both a command responsibility and a willingness to share the risks borne by the ship's company. Sandbags and other protection could reduce danger, but they did not remove the central trade-off between safety and situational awareness. Standing at the same post, he adds that the battle directed from there changed the fate of a nation and sent consequences through the following century.

evidence.note: He explains that the exposed position maximized visual information before radar and contrasts it with a protected command room.

segments.title: Mikasa's command post as a place of decision
segments.summary: Standing at Mikasa's preserved command post, Dr. Clarke reflects on how a historical place can make the weight of command decisions feel immediate.
segments.body: After explaining the tactical reason for the position, Dr. Clarke reflects on its emotional and historical force. The preserved spot feels to him like a place where choices had unusually visible consequences. He acknowledges that this response may be shaped by his reading and imagination, rather than something the site alone can prove. Even so, the reaction illustrates why preserved warships matter as historical evidence and as places of interpretation. A command post connects abstract accounts of decision-making to the physical conditions in which officers had to observe, choose, and accept risk.

evidence.note: He says the post feels like a point of decision, while acknowledging that the feeling may partly be his own interpretation.
